heading: libro dei Re, che venne Ezechia – quando giunse Rapsace, inviato dal
card_summary: This chunk continues Zorzi's extended typological commentary on water imagery drawn from Scripture, developing a threefold hierarchy of basins (bacini): the superior basin as divine blessing, the inferior basin as the blessing of the saints, and the probatica piscina of John 5 as the pool of purification for souls destined for the heavenly temple. Zorzi weaves together citations from Isaiah, Ezekiel, the Psalms, the Song of Songs, the Apocalypse, Lamentations, and the Second Book of Kings (Hezekiah's deliverance from Sennacherib's general Rabshakeh) to build an argument about purification, repentance, and the necessity of washing before entering divine presence. The chunk also introduces the Hebrew term bor (pit/well of the underworld) to interpret the "lake" imagery in Scripture as signifying hell, contrasted with the salvific pool, and cites Zechariah in connection with liberation from that pit.
essay:
Now I have the current essay. Let me expand it significantly with deeper scholarly analysis, more detailed engagement with source materials, and fuller treatment of theological and cosmological implications. I'll add 2-3 new substantive paragraphs per section while preserving all blockquotes, citations, and formatting.

## Expanded Essay

## Overview

Chunk 60 of *De Harmonia Mundi* falls within the Primo Cantico, Tono Settimo, and forms part of Zorzi's sustained meditation on water, purification, and the hierarchical structure of divine blessing. The passage moves through several interconnected typological registers — royal history, liturgical practice, sacramental theology, penitential piety, and eschatology — all organised around the symbolic contrast between life-giving pools (*piscinae*) and death-dealing pits (*laci*, Hebrew *bor*). What distinguishes Zorzi's treatment from conventional scriptural exegesis is his integration of Neoplatonic emanationist philosophy with Kabbalistic numerology and Hebrew lexical analysis, creating a synthetic theological vision in which water serves simultaneously as historical type, liturgical sign, mystical reality, and cosmological principle. The passage demonstrates the Renaissance Hermetic sensibility in which the material and spiritual orders are homologous manifestations of a single divine harmony, accessible through multiple interpretive registers.

## The Hezekiah Episode as Penitential Type

The chunk opens mid-sentence, referring to the episode from the Second Book of Kings (19:35) when Rabshakeh (*Rapsace* in the Italian), the general of the Assyrian king Sennacherib, came to threaten Hezekiah and the people of Jerusalem. Zorzi reads Hezekiah's response as the paradigm of authentic penitence: by turning to God, Hezekiah obtained the "water of grace and divine blessing," and was delivered when 185,000 Assyrian soldiers were struck down in the camp. This victory is not attributed to military might but to spiritual transformation — penitence opening the conduit through which divine grace flows into the historical order. The upper basin (*bacino superiore*) in Zorzi's schema represents divine blessing dispensed directly from God; the lower basin (*bacino inferiore*) represents the blessing of the saints, which flows downward from what it has received from above. This two-tier structure of benediction is characteristic of Zorzi's emanationist cosmology, in which higher realities overflow into lower ones in a Neoplatonic cascade.

The theoretical foundation for this hierarchical blessing derives from the Neoplatonic metaphysics that Zorzi absorbed through Marsilio Ficino and Pico della Mirandola. In Plotinus's *Enneads* (particularly tractate V.1), the One radiates reality outward in successive emanations, each lower level participating in the perfection of higher levels while remaining ontologically subordinate. Zorzi transposes this schema into Christian theology: the divine nature (*bacino superiore*) eternally overflows with blessing, which the angelic and human orders (*bacino inferiore*) receive and transmit further downward. What prevents this blessing from reaching creatures is not a lack of supply but a blockage in the conduit — namely, sin, which severs communion with the source. Hezekiah's historical penitence thus illustrates a metaphysical truth: return to God (repentance) realigns the soul with the hierarchical flow of grace. The deliverance from Sennacherib becomes an eschatological sign, proving that fidelity to the divine order conquers all temporal powers. Zorzi's reading transforms the narrative from political-military history into cosmic theology.

The Hezekiah typology also connects to Zorzi's broader treatment of kingship as a theurgic office. In *De Harmonia Mundi*, the legitimate ruler is one who maintains harmony between earthly and celestial orders, mediating divine will into temporal affairs through virtuous action. Hezekiah becomes the type of the Christian king — not by force of arms alone, but through spiritual alignment with the divine source. This resonates with medieval and Renaissance theories of sacral kingship but amplified through Neoplatonic and Kabbalistic frameworks. The 185,000 soldiers struck down represent the overcoming of the demonic powers (often numbered and ranked in Renaissance angelology) through divine intervention triggered by human repentance. In this reading, the historical victory is shadow and sign of the cosmic victory accomplished through Christ's passion and resurrection — the ultimate penitential offering that reconciles all creation to its divine source.

## The Probatica Piscina and the Necessity of Washing

Zorzi then introduces the *vasca per lavare* (pool for washing), identified with the Probatica Piscina of John 5:2 — "Vi è a Gerusalemme una vasca probatica" — where the sheep destined for temple sacrifice were washed. Nothing impure may enter the true temple of the Lord, Zorzi insists, citing Christ's words: "If I do not wash you, you will have no part with me." This transitions into a dense cento of scriptural citations concerning purification:

> Non habebis partem mecum, sicut etiam per Isaiam dicitur: Sanctus vocabitur omnis, cuius nomen scriptum fuerit in libro vitae in Ierusalem, et hoc, si abluerit Dominum sordes filiarum Sion.

<cite>p. 979 / fol. CXLIIr</cite>

Even those sheep destined for the right hand of the judge, whose names are written in the Book of Life, must still be washed in this pool. Zorzi anchors this claim with Psalm 51 ("Wash me thoroughly from my iniquity"), Ezekiel 16:9 ("I washed you with water and cleansed you"), and the Apocalypse: "He washed us from our sins in his blood" (Apoc. 1:5), and the beatitude of Apocalypse 22:14 for those who wash their robes in the blood of the Lamb.

> Beati qui lavant stolas suas in sanguine agni, ut sit potestas eorum in ligno vitae, et per portas intrent in civitatem.

<cite>p. 980 / fol. CXLIIr–141v</cite>

The blood of Christ thus operates within the same pool as the water — indeed, it washes more thoroughly (*melius abluens quam aqua*). This convergence of water and blood reflects the Johannine theology of 1 John 5:6–8 implicit in Zorzi's framework.

The Probatica Piscina passage from John's Gospel merits closer examination in light of Zorzi's typological strategy. John 5:2–9 recounts the healing of the paralytic at this pool, traditionally associated with the Pool of Bethesda. The pool had "five porticos" (consistent with first-century Jerusalem archaeology), and a multitude of the infirm — the blind, lame, paralyzed — waited there, hoping the waters would stir and bring healing. The angel descending to trouble the waters becomes in Zorzi's exegesis a type of the Holy Spirit moving upon the waters (as in Genesis 1:2), bringing life-giving power into the material world. The sheep destined for sacrifice in the temple required ritual washing (*tallal*) in Jewish law, rendering them fit for the altar. But Zorzi allegorizes this: the "sheep" are souls approaching the heavenly temple; the washing is sanctification; and the Probatica becomes a type of the sacraments (especially baptism and penance) through which Christian souls are ritually cleansed.

What is theologically crucial here is Zorzi's insistence that election (*nomen scriptum in libro vitae*) does not dispense with the necessity of purification. This may seem paradoxical: if one is already predestined to salvation, why the washing? But Zorzi's reasoning is consistent with medieval sacramental theology, particularly the via moderna's treatment of the will and grace. Predestination is not a mechanical predetermination but God's foreknowledge of human cooperation with grace. Even the "elect sheep" must undergo the ritual conformity that aligns their will with the divine order. The washing is not magically efficacious per se but signifies and effects the soul's interior consent to its transformation. In Zorzi's Neoplatonic language, the soul must be *habituated* to receive the divine influx: purification removes the obstacles (the rust and defilement of sin) that prevent the soul from participating fully in the harmonic order of grace.

The appearance of blood alongside water intensifies this sacramental signification. In the Johannine theology that Zorzi here invokes, Christ's blood poured out on Golgotha becomes the ultimate offering that renders the heavenly temple's altar perfected and eternal. The Epistle to the Hebrews (particularly chs. 9–10) articulates this: the Levitical sacrifices and ablutions were "shadows of good things to come," preparatory rituals pointing toward the final, unrepeatable sacrifice of Christ. But Zorzi goes further: the blood and water that flow from Christ's pierced side (John 19:34) are not merely historical facts but cosmic principles. The water represents the outpouring of grace (the Holy Spirit, often symbolized by water in patristic theology); the blood represents the perfect love-sacrifice through which the divine and human orders are reconciled. Both together constitute the single reality of redemption, accessible to each soul through the sacraments of the Church. In the mystical theology of the *passio Christi*, the believer "washes" in these waters through meditation and identification with Christ's suffering, thereby undergoing a second baptism of love and remorse.

## Tears as an Interior Piscina

Zorzi makes a striking turn inward: the pool may also be constituted *within ourselves* ("in nobis ipsis"), when, touched by God, we are moved to tears. He cites the Song of Songs 7:4 — "Your eyes are like the pools of Heshbon" — and glosses the toponym: "Heshbon signifies thought" (*hesbon enim cogitationem significat*), specifically the self-examining thought of Hezekiah in Isaiah 38:15: "I will reflect on all my years in the bitterness of my soul." From such recollection flow the cleansing tears of Psalm 6:6 — "Every night I will wash my bed; I will drench my couch with my tears."

> Oculi tui sicut piscinae Hesbon; Hesbon enim cogitationem significat, illam utique de qua dicebat Ezechias: Recogitabo tibi omnes annos meos in amaritudine animae meae.

<cite>p. 980 / fol. 141v</cite>

This move is characteristic of Zorzi's integrative method: the exterior, cosmological pool and the interior, psychological pool are homologous structures, both sites of purification operating within the same divine economy.

The internalization of the Probatica into tears represents a major hermeneutical shift, one aligned with the affective mysticism that flourished in the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries. Medieval commentators on the Song of Songs — particularly Bernard of Clairvaux and his successors in the Cistercian tradition — interpreted the "eyes" as the soul's capacity for contemplation (*contemplatio*), and the "pools of Heshbon" as the mind (*cognitio*) in which spiritual truth is reflected and known. Zorzi's gloss extends this: the eyes become pools because tears are the outward and sacramental sign of an interior transformation. The literal weeping of penitence (which medieval monastic tradition emphasized as a primary mark of authentic remorse) becomes a mirror in which the soul sees both its sinfulness and the divine mercy that transcends that sinfulness. In Hezekiah's case, the passage cited is Isaiah 38:15, part of the king's recovery prayer after his illness and deliverance from death. "I will reflect on all my years in the bitterness of my soul" (*Recogitabo tibi omnes annos meos in amaritudine animae meae*) — this retrospective examination of conscience in the face of mortality becomes the occasion for grace. The soul, confronted with its finitude and failings, opens itself to divine transformation.

The Neoplatonic framework underlying this passage further clarifies the imagery. In Plotinian mysticism, the soul achieves union with the One through a two-fold movement: first, an ascent of intellectual intuition in which the fragmented multiplicity of the lower world is left behind, and the soul approaches the transcendent unity; second, a descent or return into temporal existence, now transformed and bearing the imprint of what it has touched above. The "tears of Heshbon" correspond to this second moment — the soul's return into embodied, emotional consciousness, now saturated with awareness of its inferiority to and dependence upon the divine. The physical tears are the overflow of this reorientation: what the eye perceives (the soul's own condition against the infinite horizon of God) precipitates in emotional acknowledgment. Thus the interior *piscina* is not merely psychological sentiment but ontological recalibration — the soul restructuring itself in light of divine transcendence. This aligns with late medieval *affectus* theology, in which proper feeling (*affectio*) toward God is understood as a mode of knowledge and participation in grace.

The connection between tears and the Probatica further deepens when we note that both pools function as interfaces between chaos and order, death and life. The Probatica healed the sick and infirm; tears heal the soul of its spiritual sickness. Both require patience and faith — the infirm waiting for the angel to trouble the waters, the penitent waiting for the Spirit to move through their consciousness. And both are ultimately inefficacious without faith and the presence of Christ himself: John 5 makes clear that many gathered at the pool, but only one was healed (the paralytic whom Jesus addressed), suggesting that the healing is not the pool's inherent power but Christ's redemptive presence and word. Similarly, tears themselves are not salvific per se; rather, they express the soul's openness to Christ's transforming touch. The tears thus become sacramental sign — not mechanically effective, but efficacious through the faith and intention they embody.

## The Lake as Infernal Pit: Hebrew *Bor*

The final movement of the chunk introduces a sharp typological contrast. Against the salvific *piscina*, Zorzi sets the *lacus* — a stagnant, deadly body of water. He draws on Apocalypse 14:19–20 (the great winepress of God's wrath), Lamentations 3:53 (Jeremiah lamenting over Jerusalem: "They put my life into a pit"), and Isaiah 14:15 for the identification of the lake with hell.

> Ad infernum detraheris in latera putei, pro quo nos habemus in profundum laci.

<cite>p. 981</cite>

Crucially, Zorzi introduces the Hebrew term *bor* (בּוֹר), meaning "pit" or "cistern," which in Latin Bibles is rendered as *lacus* (lake) or *puteus* (well/pit). He argues that wherever Scripture speaks of a *lacus* in negative contexts, the Hebrew original has *bor*, signifying the pit of hell. Psalm 88:7 ("They have placed me in the lower pit") confirms this reading. In the supernal realm, Zorzi notes, there is no corresponding image for the *lacus*, since nothing inauspicious exists there — though there is a tribunal (*tribunal*) from which judgment is rendered, dispatching souls to the pit, where ministers bind and torment them either permanently or temporally until liberation. The chunk ends with an allusion to Zechariah (9:11) as the prophet who addresses the Saviour and Liberator from the pit — a messianic horizon that will presumably be developed in the next passage.

Zorzi's deployment of Hebrew linguistic analysis here exemplifies Renaissance philological sophistication and represents a critical turn toward textual realism against medieval allegorical excess. By insisting that *lacus* in the Vulgate translates Hebrew *bor*, Zorzi grounds his typology in the semantic specificity of the original language. This reflects the humanist recovery of Hebrew studies in the fifteenth century, particularly through scholars like Johannes Reuchlin, whose *De verbo mirifico* (1494) and *De arte cabalistica* (1517) positioned Hebrew not as a dead liturgical language but as the sacred tongue in which divine reality was originally encoded. For Zorzi, Hebrew letters and words carry metaphysical weight: the sound *bor* does not merely denote a pit but resonates with the ontological reality of depthlessness, emptiness, and severance from the divine light that emanates from above. This is not mere linguistic superstition but part of a coherent Renaissance Hermetic philosophy in which language and reality participate in a continuous fabric of correspondences.

The Kabbalistic tradition, which Zorzi is here implicitly invoking, taught that the Hebrew alphabet and the divine names are instruments of creation. The twenty-two letters of the Hebrew alphabet are held to correspond to the twenty-two paths of the sefirotic tree, and each letter carries both literal and mystical significance. The term *bor* (בּוֹר), composed of the letters beth (ב, "house"), vav (ו, "nail" or "hook"), and resh (ר, "head"), might be read by Kabbalists as signifying a "house of hooks" or a place where consciousness (resh, the "head") is bound and inverted. While Zorzi does not here provide explicit Kabbalistic etymology, his approach to Hebrew phonetics and semantics is consistent with Kabbalistic hermeneutics, which treat every letter and word as a gateway to mystical meaning. The *bor* becomes not merely a metaphorical hell but the inversion of the hierarchical flow of grace: whereas the *piscinae* enable ascent and participation in the divine overflow, the *bor* represents the soul's inversion, plunging into separation and multiplicity without the integrating principle of divine unity.

The eschatological dimension of this passage reaches toward Zorzi's larger vision of cosmic reconciliation. The fact that torment in the pit is "either permanent or temporal" (*aut perpetuo, aut pro tempore*) indicates Zorzi's acceptance of the possibility of eventual liberation even from infernal states — a position influenced by Origen's *apocatastasis* (universal restoration) and maintained in Neoplatonic Christian mysticism through Gregory of Nyssa and Maximus the Confessor. If the divine overflow is infinite and the goal is the restoration of all things in Christ (as Ephesians 1:10 suggests), then even the deepest descent into the *bor* is ultimately encompassed within the divine mercy and capable of reversal. The *tribunal* that judges and sends souls to the pit is an aspect of divine justice, but it functions within an economy of redemption rather than pure retribution. Zechariah 9:11 — the closing scriptural reference — speaks of the covenant God makes with His people and of the captives released from the "pit (*bor*) in which there is no water." The phrase "without water" is crucial: it suggests deprivation of the life-giving grace symbolized by the *piscinae*. The Messiah's role, then, is to restore those imprisoned in waterless pits to communion with the life-giving waters of the divine order.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk exemplifies Zorzi's characteristic method of layering Neoplatonic emanationism onto biblical typology. The hierarchy of basins (divine blessing → saints' blessing → purificatory pool) maps onto the sefirotic and angelic chains that structure *De Harmonia Mundi* throughout. The Hebrew term *bor* is one of several instances in the work where Zorzi demonstrates kabbalistic lexical competence to unlock a typological meaning invisible in Latin translation. The contrast between the life-giving *piscina* and the death-dealing *lacus/bor* also belongs to Zorzi's broader cosmological mapping of "above" and "below," ascent and descent, in which purification is the necessary condition for participation in the harmonic order of the divine world.

The theological anthropology implicit in this passage is particularly important for understanding Zorzi's vision of human spiritual transformation. The soul is understood as naturally intermediate between the eternal and the temporal, capable of orientation toward either pole. Purification (whether through the external Probatica, the interior tears of Heshbon, or the sacramental washing in Christ's blood) is the process by which the soul re-orients itself toward the divine source, aligning its will and affections with the harmonic flow of grace. This is neither purely forensic justification (in the later Lutheran sense) nor purely mystical absorption, but a theological vision of theosis — the gradual transformation and divinization of the human person through participatory union with divine grace. The *piscinae* become instruments of this theosis, and the warning against the *bor* serves as a negative theology: to refuse purification is to invert one's orientation, to plunge from potential ascent into actual descent.

Moreover, this passage illuminates Zorzi's integration of liturgical practice into his systematic theology. The temple, its priestly services, the washing of sacrificial animals, the great festivals — these are not merely historical institutions now superseded by Christianity, but living types that structure Christian practice perpetually. The Christian who receives baptism enters into the reality that the Probatica prefigured; the penitent who sheds tears of contrition participates in the waters of Heshbon; the communicant who receives the Eucharist is washed in the blood of the Lamb. *De Harmonia Mundi* thus offers a vision of Christianity in which every ritual and sacrament is anchored in the eternal divine order and in which each person's spiritual journey recapitulates the cosmic drama of descent and return that constitutes the "harmony of the world." Water serves as the fundamental metaphor for this unified vision: as blessing descending from above, as the medium of purification, as the interstitial realm between heaven and hell, and as the symbol of the Spirit's vivifying presence permeating all creation. In Zorzi's hands, water becomes a master-key unlocking the hidden continuity between historical Scripture, mystical experience, and cosmic theology.
